preamble: The phonetic transformation from the alveolar lateral approximant /l/ to the voiced alveolar fricative /z/ is a documented sound change, primarily characterized by spirantization and fortition. This involves an increase in the stricture of articulation, converting an approximant into a fricative at the same place of articulation.

examples.note: Occurs as an alternation in verbal suffixes, such as the applicative /-el/ and perfective /-ile/, often triggered by the short causative /-y/ or the root-final consonant.
A historical shift from Proto-Ao, occurring before a high front vowel with a velar coda (*-ik/ŋ).

sources: Bi, L. (2023). Causatives, Spirantization, and Perfective Allomorphy in Silozi. Studies in African Linguistics, 52(1).
Hyman, L. & Merrill, J. (2015). Morphology, irregularity, and Bantu frication: The case of Lulamogi. UC Berkeley PhonLab Annual Report, 11(11).
Chelliah, S. L. (2008). A Phonological Reconstruction of Proto-Central Naga. UC Berkeley Linguistics.
